Tafseer:
The eloquence of the word 'Iqra' in the sense of reciting to deliver the call:
The word 'Iqra' (recite) does not merely mean reading—as a teacher says to his student—but also means reciting to deliver the call to others, or communicating the message to them. This word is used in this sense in various places in the Qur'an. For example, addressing the disbelievers who tried to prevent the recitation of the Qur'an, He states:
(So when the Qur'an is recited, listen to it and pay attention that you may receive mercy) (Al-A'raf, 7:204),
and in Surah Al-Isra:
(And when you recite the Qur'an, We place between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a hidden barrier) (Al-Isra, 17:45).
Context indicates that the word is used in this very sense here.
A highly important warning: 'In the name of your Lord Who created' (bismi rabbika-lladhi khalaq) - That is: recite this Qur'an to the people in the name of your Lord, Who is the Creator of the universe. This is a highly important warning. It is commanded: present this as the obligatory command of your Lord so that people know that the speech recited to them is directly the speech of the Lord of the worlds. It is neither the speech of the caller nor of any other person, nor is it a human request that can be easily rejected. Rather, it is the speech of the Creator and Owner Who has the right to command His creation, and whose duty is to obey Him without hesitation. No one should dare to ignore it, mock it, or oppose it as a minor thing.
It should noted that the Holy Qur'an is directly the speech of Allah. No previous book holds this honor of being entirely composed of the words revealed by Allah Almighty. For this reason, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed to present it in His name to clarify its true grandeur, warning the Quraysh of the consequences of opposing it. The prophecies in the ancient scriptures also stated: 'Whatever he speaks, he will speak in the name of God, and Allah Almighty will take retribution from those who reject His words.' Thus, these words clarify the grandeur of the Qur'an and warn the Quraysh to reflect on the consequences of their opposition.

This is a specific mention after a general mention. The first verse mentioned the creation of the entire universe; this verse mentions the creation of man with special care.
The purpose of reminding man of his initial stages of creation: 'Alaq' means a clinging clot or congealed blood. Reminding man of his initial stages of creation is common in the Qur'an—such as in Surah Al-Hajj, Surah Al-Mu'minun, Surah As-Sajdah, Surah Al-Qiyamah, and Surah Ad-Dahr. We have explained the important words and the purpose of this reminder. It is generally intended to point to three realities:
- First, the God Whose power and wisdom can turn a clinging clot of blood into a rational, hearing, and seeing human being, what difficulty can He face in recreating him after death?
- Second, the power and wisdom manifest in human creation are proofs that he was not created in vain, but a Day of account must inevitably come for him to receive reward or punishment.
- Third, a human created from such an insignificant and low element should not boast of his purity or hereditary pride, nor display arrogance.
While some places point to all three realities, some focus on one or two. Here, context indicates that it points to the first two realities—showing that the Messenger should communicate His speech in His name and remind them that the Creator Who turned a clinging clot into a human is powerful over recreating him to take his account.

A great favor to the Arabs: This second 'Iqra' serves as an exchange (badal) for the first 'Iqra', reinforcing the command given earlier. However, it also highlights the aspect of bestowing favor—showing that the Quraysh should appreciate Allah's great favor in arranging their guidance through the pen. Note that before this, the Children of Ishmael had some oral traditions of Abraham and Ishmael's teachings, which had become corrupted with the passage of time. The teachings of other prophets were also oral. Moses was given the Ten Commandments in writing, but the present Torah consists of written traditions. It is impossible to distinguish between the actual words of Allah and those of unknown narrators. But for the Qur'an, Allah Almighty arranged that its every word is His direct speech, preserved in writing rather than oral traditions under His guidance, as explained in Surah Al-Qalam and Surah Al-Qiyam, ensuring no alteration can occur.

Further favor and a warning: This special arrangement is pointed to by: 'Who taught by the pen' (alladhi 'allama bil-qalam). This was indeed a great favor to the Arabs. First, such an arrangement for preserving the revelation was never made for any previous nation. Second, being unlettered, they were unfamiliar with the use of the pen, but through the Qur'an, they preserved this great heavenly treasury which is a source of life for them and for the entire world.

This is another aspect of His favor: He did not merely teach them by the pen, but also taught them what they knew not. Although 'man' (insan) is general, the primary addressees were the unlettered Arabs. It is stated: Allah, out of His special favor, revealed His perfect guidance to bring them out of the darkness of ignorance. They are obligated to appreciate it, as stated in Surah Al-Jumu'ah, verse 2:
(He is the One Who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from themselves reciting to them His verses and purifying them and teaching them the Book and wisdom, although they were before in clear error) (Al-Jumu'ah, 62:2).
This same has appeared in Al-Baqarah: 151, 198, and Al-Imran: 164.
This verse, along with bestowing favor, contains a warning to the Quraysh: if they do not appreciate His greatest blessing, let them reflect on the consequence of their ungratefulness and transgression!

This describes the attitude they chose regarding this guidance. It is stated: instead of appreciating this mercy, they are rejecting it with rebelliousness, because having received wealth and status, they regard themselves as independent of God.
The attitude of the Quraysh: Beginning this verse with 'No!' (Kalla) is to refute the fabrications the Quraysh raised to deny the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Qur'an.

It is stated: their fabrications are merely to cover up the reality. The actual cause of their denial is their independence of God and love of the world, but they pretend to raise some doubts for which they find no satisfying answer.

'Ruj'a' is a verbal noun on the pattern of 'bushra', meaning return.
Warning and reminding of the Hereafter: It is stated: let them do what they do, but ultimately their return is to your Lord, of Whom they are untroubled. At that moment, the reality of their transgression will be clear to them. If they assume their partners will protect them, their delusion will be exposed. On that Day, the sovereignty will belong only to Allah, and no one will save them from His grasp.

An example of the transgression of the Quraysh's wicked leaders: This describes an example of their transgression. It is asked: 'Have you seen the one who forbids a servant when he prays?' This refers to those wicked leaders of the Quraysh who prevented the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and other Muslims from praying. The primary right of the Creator on His servant is worship, and the foremost status in worship belongs to prayer; therefore, one who prays is performing his primary duty, deserving respect and emulation. If someone dares to prevent him, he is preventing him from his primary duty and God's primary right.
We explained 'Have you seen' (ara'ayta) on several occasions—showing that it is used to draw attention to someone's highly inappropriate action, just as we say: 'Look at him!'
'The one' (alladhi) does not necessarily refer to a specific individual, but represents anyone who commits such a foolish act. The Prophet was prevented from praying not only by Abu Jahl, but also by other wicked leaders, and they committed such bad behavior not only against the Prophet but also against other Muslims.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9 and 10 was combined in the original text.]

The necessity of reflecting before action: That is: before committing such a mad action, he should have reflected: his hostility should not blind him to his own end. There is a possibility that this servant of Allah is upon guidance and is showing the path of piety, and he is inviting his own ruin by preventing him! The meaning is: on what basis did he assume himself to be right in committing what belongs to the work of Satan!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11 and 12 was combined in the original text.]

Referencing the second possibility: This refers to the second possibility. It is asked: 'What if he denies and turns away!' meaning: then he has opened the door of Hell for himself with his own hands! Note the Arabic rule where the response to a conditional clause is omitted because its severity is beyond description. The response is omitted here for this reason. A similar example appeared in Surah Yunus.
The end of those who deny the Messenger and turn away from his call was described in Surah Al-Layl as:
(None will burn therein except the most wretched, who denied and turned away) (Al-Layl, 92:15-16).

That is: does he not know that Allah is ever observing his transgressions? Since He is observing, He will surely take retribution. He is Just, Merciful, Mighty, and Jealous. He cannot tolerate His servants being prevented from His worship, and He will not remain a silent spectator.

Severe warning to the rebels: This is a highly severe warning to such rebels. It is stated: if he does not desist, We will drag him by the forelock. 'Nasiyah' means the forelock. 'Saf'' means to seize something firmly and drag it. In Surah Ar-Rahman, He states:
(So they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet) (Ar-Rahman, 55:41).

This is an exchange (badal) for 'the forelock' (an-nasiyah). Although one is definite and the other indefinite, an indefinite noun followed by an attribute can serve as an exchange for a definite noun. Note the intense wrath manifest in every word of this verse. Their forelock is described as: 'a lying, sinning forelock!' The face and forelock represent a person's identity; hence, they are used to refer to his entire personality. The greatest honor for a person's forelock is the mark of prostration. If someone is so wretched that he neither prostrates himself nor lets others prostrate, he deserves to be dragged by his forelock and cast into Hell.

Challenging the rebels: This is a challenge to these rebels: if anyone is proud of his power and population, let him call his associates, and We will call Our guardians, and see who possesses more strength! This challenge was tested at the Battle of Badr, and everyone saw how the power of the Quraysh vanished before the hosts of God.
'Nadi' literally means an assembly or society, referring here to the individuals bound by tribal unity. In this context, it means his associates or party.

'Zabaniyah' is the plural of 'zabniyah', which literally means defenders, but is used for police and guards. In this context, it means 'the guardians'. These are the angels of the divine task force who are sent for specific, temporary missions.

Instruction to the Prophet: It is stated: if any rebel prevents you from prostrating before God, pay no attention to his action, but prostrate and draw near to your Lord. Note that the Qur'an has repeatedly declared prayer to be the key to patience, resolve, and victory, and prostration is the highest expression of prayer. This points to the reality: who can dare to prevent you from what is the purpose of your life and the only means to connect with God? If anyone dares to do so, seek His protection, and the only way to do so is to fall in prostration before Him.
